keyPoints: The /etc/passwd file stores essential user account information like username, UID, GID, home directory, and login shell.
The /etc/shadow file stores the encrypted password and related security information like password aging policies.
The passwd command allows a user to change their own password, while root can change any user's password.
commonMistakes: Attempting to directly edit the /etc/passwd file to change a password instead of using the 'passwd' command.
Forgetting that /etc/passwd must be world-readable, so sensitive data like passwords is stored in the more secure /etc/shadow file.
Confusing the User ID (UID) with the Group ID (GID) when examining an entry in the /etc/passwd file.
